macro_segments.title: Knowledge of Impurity (Yedi'ot HaTumah): The Dispute over ונעלם (Vene'elam)

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishnah: Three conditions for liability regarding tumah
Rabbi Akiva's interpretation: Vene'elam appears twice, deriving two yedi'ot
Rabbi Yishmael's challenge: Vene'elam alone implies prior knowledge
Rava's linguistic proof: Vene'elam in the context of sotah contradicts knowing then forgetting
The sotah case analyzed: The woman's husband must be innocent for the procedure to work
Further examples from Torah: Divine wisdom and hidden laws cannot mean prior knowledge
Abaye's reinterpretation: Yedi'ah means knowledge of the concept, not specific awareness
Rashi's explanation: The boy learned that tumah defiles but didn't consciously realize he became tamei
Rav Pappa's question: What about cases with no initial knowledge?
The case of tinok shenishba: A child raised among non-Jews lacks basic knowledge entirely
Historical context: Knowledge and observance in different periods of Jewish history

macro_segments.title: Shabbat Exits (Yetziot HaShabbat): The Permutations of Transfer Between Domains

micro_segments.title: Introducing the Mishnah: Two that are four inside and two that are four outside
The three components of prohibited transfer: Akirah, hanacha, and movement between domains
Case 1: Poor man extends hand inside; homeowner places object in his hand
Case 2: Poor man extends hand inside; homeowner takes object from his hand
Case 3: Homeowner extends hand outside; poor man places object in his hand
Case 4: Homeowner extends hand outside; poor man takes object from his hand
Cases 5-8: Mirror images where neither party completes the transfer alone
Gemara's question: Why does our Mishnah say only two that are four, unlike Shabbat?
Preliminary answer: Shabbat details both chiyuvin and paturin; Shevuot details only chiyuvin
Rav Pappa's objection: How can yetziot be only two cases if all eight are chiyuvin?
Resolution: Yetziot technically refers to two cases, but we count hachnasa as yetziah for breadth
Source: The Baraita's formula 'hamotzi meirshut lereshut' encompasses both removal and bringing in
Rav Ashi's explanation: We call hachnasa and yetziah both yetziah because they are transfer acts
Raba's interpretation: Yetziot really means reshuyot—domains—not literal acts

macro_segments.title: Appearances of Nega'im (Leprosy Marks): Classifying Four Marks into Two Categories

micro_segments.title: Introduction to nega'im: Baheret, se'et, sid heichal, and krum habeitza
Visual comparison: Baheret (snow-white), se'et (wool-white), sid (lime-white), krum (eggshell-white)
The ambiguity in the Mishnah: Does sapachat attach to baheret alone or to both baheret and se'et?
Rabbi Akiva's reading: Baheret and se'et are avot; sid and krum are secondary colors
Hanina's challenge: If Rabbi Akiva holds this way, sid heichal cannot combine with anything
The combination rules: Secondary colors can combine only with their parent category
Resolution: Sapachat is secondary to se'et by Torah; sid heichal remains problematic
Final clarification: The Mishnah's language is not Rabbi Akiva; it uses a different classification

topical_tags.term: ידיעה בתחילה וידיעה בסוף (yedi'ah batchilah v'yedi'ah basof) — knowledge at beginning and end
עקירה והנחה (akirah v'hanachah) — lifting and placing down
רשות היחיד / רשות הרבים (reshut hayachid / reshut harabim) — private/public domain
Rabbi Akiva vs. other Tannaim on nega'im classification
בהרת, שאת, סיד, קרום ביצה (baheret, se'et, sid, krum habeitza) — four types of leprosy marks
אבות ותולדות (avot v'toldot) — primary and secondary categories
מצטרף (mitztaref) — combining measures for minimum size
טומאה וטהרה (tumah v'taharah) — impurity and purity
